**John Rubinstein** (born December 8, 1946) is an American actor, composer and director.[[1]](#cite_note-NYT-1)[[2]](#cite_note-NYTF-2)

## Early life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_Rubinstein&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life")]

Rubinstein is the son of [Polish](/wiki/Polish_people "Polish people") parents. His mother, Aniela ([née](/wiki/Married_and_maiden_names "Married and maiden names") Młynarska), a dancer and writer, was a Roman Catholic native of [Warsaw](/wiki/Warsaw,_Poland "Warsaw, Poland"), the daughter of conductor [Emil Młynarski](/wiki/Emil_M%C5%82ynarski "Emil Młynarski"). His father was [Polish-Jewish](/wiki/History_of_the_Jews_in_Poland "History of the Jews in Poland") concert pianist [Arthur Rubinstein](/wiki/Arthur_Rubinstein "Arthur Rubinstein").[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6) He attended [El Rodeo Public School](/wiki/Beverly_Hills_Unified_School_District#El_Rodeo_Elementary_School "Beverly Hills Unified School District") in Beverly Hills (K–2), Cours La Cascade in Paris, France (1954), and St. Bernard's School (3–8) and [Collegiate School (New York City)](/wiki/Collegiate_School_(New_York_City) "Collegiate School (New York City)") (9–12) in New York City. He studied theater and music at the [University of California, Los Angeles](/wiki/University_of_California,_Los_Angeles "University of California, Los Angeles") (1964–1967), and later composition at the [Juilliard](/wiki/Juilliard_School "Juilliard School") School in New York.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

## Career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_Rubinstein&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Career")]

### Theater

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_Rubinstein&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Theater")]

Rubinstein made his Broadway acting debut in 1972 and received a [Theatre World Award](/wiki/Theatre_World_Award "Theatre World Award") for creating the title role in the musical *[Pippin](/wiki/Pippin_(musical) "Pippin (musical)")*, directed by [Bob Fosse](/wiki/Bob_Fosse "Bob Fosse").[[1]](#cite_note-NYT-1) In 1980 he won the [Tony](/wiki/Tony_Award "Tony Award"), [Drama Desk](/wiki/Drama_Desk_Award "Drama Desk Award"), Los Angeles Drama Critics Circle, and [Drama-Logue Awards](/wiki/Drama-Logue_Award "Drama-Logue Award") for his portrayal of James Leeds in [Mark Medoff](/wiki/Mark_Medoff "Mark Medoff")'s *[Children of a Lesser God](/wiki/Children_of_a_Lesser_God_(play) "Children of a Lesser God (play)")*,[[1]](#cite_note-NYT-1) directed by [Gordon Davidson](/wiki/Gordon_Davidson_(director) "Gordon Davidson (director)").

Other Broadway appearances were in [Neil Simon](/wiki/Neil_Simon "Neil Simon")'s *[Fools](/wiki/Fools_(play) "Fools (play)")*, directed by [Mike Nichols](/wiki/Mike_Nichols "Mike Nichols"), and [Herman Wouk](/wiki/Herman_Wouk "Herman Wouk")'s *[The Caine Mutiny Court-Martial](/wiki/The_Caine_Mutiny_Court-Martial_(play) "The Caine Mutiny Court-Martial (play)")*, which earned him a Drama Desk nomination; he replaced [William Hurt](/wiki/William_Hurt "William Hurt") as Eddie in [David Rabe](/wiki/David_Rabe "David Rabe")'s *[Hurlyburly](/wiki/Hurlyburly "Hurlyburly")*, replaced [David Dukes](/wiki/David_Dukes "David Dukes") in [David Henry Hwang](/wiki/David_Henry_Hwang "David Henry Hwang")'s *[M. Butterfly](/wiki/M._Butterfly "M. Butterfly")*, and starred in *[Getting Away with Murder](/wiki/Getting_Away_with_Murder_(play) "Getting Away with Murder (play)")*, by [Stephen Sondheim](/wiki/Stephen_Sondheim "Stephen Sondheim") and [George Furth](/wiki/George_Furth "George Furth"), directed by [Jack O'Brien](/wiki/Jack_O%27Brien_(director) "Jack O'Brien (director)"), and *[Ragtime](/wiki/Ragtime_(musical) "Ragtime (musical)")*, by [Terrence McNally](/wiki/Terrence_McNally "Terrence McNally"), [Stephen Flaherty](/wiki/Stephen_Flaherty "Stephen Flaherty"), and [Lynn Ahrens](/wiki/Lynn_Ahrens "Lynn Ahrens"). In 2014, he joined the Broadway cast of the hit revival of *[Pippin](/wiki/Pippin_(musical) "Pippin (musical)")*, directed by [Diane Paulus](/wiki/Diane_Paulus "Diane Paulus"), this time playing Pippin's father, Charlemagne; and subsequently repeated that role on the national tour throughout the United States and Japan in 2014–15.

In 2016, it was announced that Rubinstein would join fellow Tony-winner [Christian Borle](/wiki/Christian_Borle "Christian Borle") in [Charlie and the Chocolate Factory](/wiki/Charlie_and_the_Chocolate_Factory_(musical) "Charlie and the Chocolate Factory (musical)") as Grandpa Joe, directed by Jack O'Brien.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

In 2023 he starred in the one man biographical show *Eisenhower: This Piece of Ground* debuting [Off-Broadway](/wiki/Off-Broadway "Off-Broadway") at the St. Clemens Theatre in [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City") and subsequently touring around the US. The show portrays the former president at his home in retirement, working on his second autobiography, reminiscing about his life and musing about his popularity and how he will be remembered.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

He taught a course in musical theater audition techniques at the [University of Southern California](/wiki/University_of_Southern_California "University of Southern California"), and directed their spring musicals.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

### Film and television

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_Rubinstein&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Film and television")]

Rubinstein's feature films include *[21 Grams](/wiki/21_Grams "21 Grams")*, *[Red Dragon](/wiki/Red_Dragon_(2002_film) "Red Dragon (2002 film)")*,[[2]](#cite_note-NYTF-2) *[Mercy](/wiki/Mercy_(2000_film) "Mercy (2000 film)")*, *[Another Stakeout](/wiki/Another_Stakeout "Another Stakeout")*,[[2]](#cite_note-NYTF-2) *[Someone to Watch Over Me](/wiki/Someone_to_Watch_Over_Me_(film) "Someone to Watch Over Me (film)")*,[[2]](#cite_note-NYTF-2) *[Daniel](/wiki/Daniel_(1983_film) "Daniel (1983 film)")*,[[2]](#cite_note-NYTF-2) *[The Boys from Brazil](/wiki/The_Boys_from_Brazil_(film) "The Boys from Brazil (film)")*,[[2]](#cite_note-NYTF-2) *[Rome & Jewel](/wiki/Rome_%26_Jewel "Rome & Jewel")*, *Jekyll*, *Kid Cop*, *[Getting Straight](/wiki/Getting_Straight "Getting Straight")*,[[2]](#cite_note-NYTF-2) *[Zachariah](/wiki/Zachariah_(film) "Zachariah (film)")*,[[11]](#cite_note-11) *[The Trouble with Girls](/wiki/The_Trouble_with_Girls_(film) "The Trouble with Girls (film)")*, and *[The Car](/wiki/The_Car_(1977_film) "The Car (1977 film)")*. He appears in the film *[Being the Ricardos](/wiki/Being_the_Ricardos "Being the Ricardos")* as [Jess Oppenheimer](/wiki/Jess_Oppenheimer "Jess Oppenheimer").[[12]](#cite_note-12)

### Voice Work
